coredata.dc:title: Enhanced visible/near-infrared light harvesting and superior charge separation via 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid architecture for photocatalytic oxygen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Siyuan
Li, Xin
Meng, Xin
Chen, Tianxing
Kong, Weiyu
Li, Yao
Zhao, Yixin
Wang, Dawei
Zhu, Shenmin
Cheema, Waqas Akram
Pan, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Since the water splitting rate-limiting oxygen evolution remains sluggish, engineering a rational architecture for photocatalysts to fulfill water oxidation needs becomes a vital issue. Here, we detail a 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid strategy for constructing a heterostructure of carbon dots (CDots) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) to enhance the photocatalytic water oxidation of monoclinic-BiVO4 nanosheets (CBrG). Given the visible-light-harvesting ability and up-conversion characteristics of 0D CDots, more photogenerated electron-hole pairs participated in water oxidation under visible and near-infrared light irradiation. Meanwhile, 0D CDots behaved as electron acceptors on 2D rGO to suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs. This nature licenses for the feasible electron transfer from excited m-BiVO4 to 0D CDots via electron transfer channels of 2D rGO, facilitating the separated holes to migrate onto the m-BiVO4 surface for water oxidation. Compared with the rGO decorated m-BiVO4 nanosheets (BrG), these merits endow the CBrG with an over 212% enhancement in O2 yield under visible light irradiation as well as notable O2 yield under near-infrared light irradiation, and a 1.57-fold increase in apparent quantum efficiency. The enhancement is also verified by the significant growth of ·OH radicals derived from OH−/H2O oxidation and ·OOH/·O2 − radicals originated from O2 reduction. This work paves a new way for the 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid architecture applied in solar energy conversion.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality
Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province
Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation mechanism for CBrG. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterizations of the physical structures for the BrG and the CBrG. a) SEM and c) TEM images of the BrG. Inset: the corresponding HRTEM image of the BrG. b) SEM and d) TEM images of the CBrG. Inset: the corresponding HRTEM image of the CBrG. e) A TEM image of the CDots in CBrG. f) A magnified TEM image of the selected region of e) marked in blue. Inset: the corresponding HRTEM image of a single CDot. The distributed CDots in e) and f) are highlighted by yellow circles. g) The size distribution of the CDots in CBrG from e). h) XRD patterns. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterizations of the chemical status for the BrG and the CBrG. a) XPS survey spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of b) C 1s and c) V 2p. d) Raman spectra and e) FT-IR spectra. Inset: magnification of the section indicated by a dashed box. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterizations of the optical and electronic properties for the BrG and the CBrG. a) UV–Vis absorption spectra. Inset: digital photograph of photocatalyst powders. b) (αhv)
2 versus hv curve. Two dashed lines are the tangents, and the intersection value is the optical band gap. c) XPS valence band spectra. These dashed lines mark the baselines and the tangents, and the intersection value gives the position of valence band edge. d) Experimental energy band structure diagram based on the results in b) and c). e) Digital photograph of CDots aqueous solution. f) Up-conversion PL spectra of CDots. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photocatalytic performances for the BrG and the CBrG. a) Time course of O2 yield from water oxidation under visible and near-infrared light irradiation. b) Apparent quantum efficiency irradiated by a 300 W xenon lamp with a bandpass filter of λ ± 10 nm for 420, 450, 475, 500, and 520 nm. c) Cycling stability test and d) amount of O2 yield with different CDots loading amount under visible light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Mechanism exploration for the photocatalytic process. a) Steady-state PL spectra (excitation at 340 nm) and b) time-resolved PL decay spectra (excitation at 365 nm), insets are average lifetimes. c) EIS Nyquist plots without light illumination and d) transient photocurrent responses under visible and near-infrared light irradiation, respectively. Both of measurements were carried out at a 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte (pH = 7.0) and the constant potential was 0.6 V. Electron spin resonance signals of e) ·O2 − radicals, f) ·OOH radicals and ·OH radicals in the presence of DMPO as a spin-trapping agent in the dark and under visible light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic representation of the photocatalytic mechanism over CBrG. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Enhanced visible/near-infrared light harvesting and superior charge separation via 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid architecture for photocatalytic oxygen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Metal Matrix Composites, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Metal Matrix Composites
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Metal Matrix Composites, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Particles and Catalysis Research Group, School of Chemical Engineering, University of New South Wales, Sydney, NSW, 2052, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Particles and Catalysis Research Group, School of Chemical Engineering, University of New South Wales, Sydney, NSW, 2052, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the water splitting rate-limiting oxygen evolution remains sluggish, engineering a rational architecture for photocatalysts to fulfill water oxidation needs becomes a vital issue. Here, we detail a 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid strategy for constructing a heterostructure of carbon dots (CDots) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) to enhance the photocatalytic water oxidation of monoclinic-BiVO4 nanosheets (CBrG). Given the visible-light-harvesting ability and up-conversion characteristics of 0D CDots, more photogenerated electron-hole pairs participated in water oxidation under visible and near-infrared light irradiation. Meanwhile, 0D CDots behaved as electron acceptors on 2D rGO to suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs. This nature licenses for the feasible electron transfer from excited m-BiVO4 to 0D CDots via electron transfer channels of 2D rGO, facilitating the separated holes to migrate onto the m-BiVO4 surface for water oxidation. Compared with the rGO decorated m-BiVO4 nanosheets (BrG), these merits endow the CBrG with an over 212% enhancement in O2 yield under visible light irradiation as well as notable O2 yield under near-infrared light irradiation, and a 1.57-fold increase in apparent quantum efficiency. The enhancement is also verified by the significant growth of ·OH radicals derived from OH−/H2O oxidation and ·OOH/·O2 − radicals originated from O2 reduction. This work paves a new way for the 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid architecture applied in solar energy conversion.

To optimize light-harvesting ability and charge carrier separation efficiency, we detail a 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid strategy for constructing a heterostructure of carbon dots (CDots) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) to enhance both the photocatalytic water oxidation and the molecular oxygen activation of monoclinic-BiVO4. Given the visible-light-harvesting ability and up-conversion characteristics of 0D CDots, more photogenerated electron-hole pairs participated in water oxidation under visible and near-infrared light irradiation. Meanwhile, 0D CDots behaved as electron acceptors on 2D rGO to suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With the advancement of human society, there is growing global energy demand. To this end, chemical fuels with high specific energy are regarded as very promising energy storage media. Solar energy, the inexhaustible energy, has attracted tremendous attention. Especially the utilization of solar energy to drive the thermodynamically uphill reactions to store it in chemical bonds [1,2]. Of these reactions, photocatalytic water splitting into molecular hydrogen and oxygen provides a prominent and clean method to utilize solar energy to obtain fuels [3,4]. For the water splitting process, the half-reaction of water oxidation into diatomic O2 gas has been well recognized as the rate-limiting step due to its 4H+/4e− transfer process and high activation energy barrier (≈700 mV) for O-O bond formation. Namely, the water oxidation half-reaction remains sluggish to achieve highly efficient overall water splitting [5,6]. By far intense efforts have been dedicated to developing water oxidation photocatalysts (WOPs) comprising various inorganic and organic systems, whereas the most widely studied WOPs are only a few metal oxides (e.g., WO3, TiO2, and Fe2O3, etc.) in the past decades, whose valence band (VB) is mainly composed of an O 2p orbital located around +3 VRHE and positive enough to oxidize water [3]. Nevertheless, several demerits always restrict their practical application in oxygen-generating photocatalytic systems, including inferior solar harvesting efficiency, low charge carrier mobility, slow reaction kinetics, unsatisfied apparent quantum efficiency, and so forth.

To explore highly active WOPs to accelerate the water splitting process, we should specifically address the three critical intermediate steps during photocatalysis – light absorption, charge separation and transfer, and surface catalytic reactions [7]. For the first two steps, bismuth vanadate, especially the monoclinic scheelite structure (m-BiVO4), has stood out as frontier WOPs since Kudo et al. used m-BiVO4 to achieve water oxidation under visible light irradiation [8]. Compared with other metal oxides whose VB consists only of O 2p orbitals and can only respond to ultraviolet light, the VB dominated by a hybrid orbital of Bi 6s and O 2p results in the decreased band gap of m-BiVO4, thereby substantially expanding the light absorption to the visible light region [9]. Also, the local environment at the Bi and V sites in m-BiVO4 exhibits remarkable deformation, as opposed to the tetragonal and zircon-type forms with symmetric structures. The BiO8 dodecahedron distortion causes an improved lone-pair effect of the Bi 6s orbital, and the VO4 tetrahedral distortion induces an internal electric field, boosting the separation of photogenerated carriers [10]. Hence these two merits, as well as earth-abundance and non-toxicity, make m-BiVO4 a potential candidate for photocatalysis [7,11]. In terms of surface catalytic reactions, some 2D support materials (e.g., reduced graphene oxide (rGO), C3N4 nanosheets, and metal-organic frameworks, etc.) have abundant surface properties, which can provide more possibilities to enhance the water oxidation performance of m-BiVO4 through accommodating m-BiVO4 [12]. Ideal support materials should satisfy the following features: (i) specific active centers to allow the nucleation of crystals; (ii) large surface area to support the high loading of catalytic sites; (iii) high charge carrier mobility to facilitate the photoexcited electron transfer.

Among various supports, the 2D carbon material rGO can meet the strict criteria above – offering oxygen-containing functional groups (OFGs) as nucleation centers of m-BiVO4 crystals, providing abundant reactive sites with ultra-large surface area, and possessing high conductivity to act as electron transfer channels [13,14]. Moreover, the coupling of m-BiVO4 and rGO to form a hybrid structure necessarily results in the generation of an interface that affects the electron transfer efficiency [10]. Noteworthy is that a small Mott-Schottky barrier caused by the higher Fermi level of rGO than that of m-BiVO4 favors the photoexcited electron transfer from m-BiVO4 to rGO across their contact interface [15,16]. Although the photocatalytic activity of m-BiVO4-rGO composite is indeed much higher than that of bare m-BiVO4, it still falls short of expectations including the following drawbacks: (i) limited visible light harvesting and scarce infrared light utilization; (ii) insufficient charge carrier separation efficiency. For the former, the m-BiVO4 with a fixed absorption edge can only absorb part of visible light. Considering the composition of solar irradiation spectrum (i.e., ca. 7% ultraviolet light, ca. 50% visible light and ca. 43% infrared light), how to make full use of visible light and even infrared light is a challenging issue that must be considered in the field of photocatalysis [12]. As to the latter, the electron storage ability of conjugated sp2-bonded carbon network within rGO is extremely low, which provides new opportunities for the recombination of electron-hole pairs, unless there are additional electron acceptors loaded on rGO [17]. Therefore, unearthing optimal materials that can be combined with m-BiVO4-rGO for overcoming these referred drawbacks is imperative.

In principle, light-harvesting ability and charge carrier separation efficiency can be both optimized by employing the emerging 0D quasi-spherical carbon material – carbon dots (CDots; monodisperse graphite nanoparticles less than 10 nm in diameter) – in the field of photocatalysis. CDots, first discovered in 2004 [18], consist of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and exhibit excellent light absorption property, unique up-conversion characteristics, and electron-acceptor nature [19–21]. Consequently, a series of CDots-based photocatalysts, including CDots coupled with TiO2 [22], C3N4 [23], or Bi2WO6 [24], were desirably fabricated in efforts to promote their performance for visible/near-infrared-light-driven photocatalytic reactions. These works together suggest that with CDots incorporated into m-BiVO4-rGO should be expected for encouraging improvement in the photocatalytic activity of m-BiVO4-rGO, whereas no works have been hitherto done to combine them and unveiled for the related mechanism. Hopefully, both 0D CDots and 2D rGO are rich in OFGs on each surface, providing the prerequisite of anchoring 0D CDots on 2D rGO, to form a 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid architecture with chemical bonding. Given the unique optical properties and electronic nature of CDots, we aim to tackle the intrinsic limitations of m-BiVO4-rGO via the deliberate incorporation of CDots. Using the water splitting rate-limiting oxygen evolution as a model reaction, this well-designed 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid architecture would fully exert superiority of 0D CDots with 2D support material rGO, for significantly enhancing the photocatalytic water oxidation activity of m-BiVO4-rGO.

Herein, deeming the m-BiVO4-rGO as a prototype, we first developed a 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid strategy to obtain carbon dots (CDots) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) co-decorated m-BiVO4 nanosheets (CBrG) for triggering photocatalytic water oxidation reaction. To stress the applicability and superiority of 0D CDots on 2D rGO, a systematic investigation into the physical structures, chemical status, optical and electronic properties, and photocatalytic performances was carried out, supplemented by the mechanism exploration for the photocatalytic process. As anticipated, this 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid architecture endows the CBrG with an over 212% enhancement in O2 yield under visible light irradiation and notable O2 yield under near-infrared light irradiation, as well as a 1.57-fold increase in apparent quantum efficiency relative to the counterpart BrG. The improvement is also corroborated by the significant growth of ·OH radicals derived from OH−/H2O oxidation and ·OOH/·O2 − radicals originated from O2 reduction.

All initial chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. The CDots aqueous solution was prepared by a one-step hydrothermal pathway using 560 μl of ethylene glycol and 1050 mg of citric acid as the precursors, these precursors were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and stirred for 1 h. As the solution became transparent and colorless, it was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged to obtain the yellowish CDots aqueous solution [25]. Besides, the GO sol-gel solution was prepared according to a previous study [26].

In a typical synthesis of CDots and rGO co-decorated m-BiVO4 nanosheets (CBrG), the GO sol-gel solution containing 100 mg of GO was dispersed in 90 mL of deionized water under ultrasonic treatment to create a brown suspension. Then, 631 mg of BiCl3 and 400 mg of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were dissolved into the suspension by stirring for 4 h. Next, 234 mg of NH4VO3 was dissolved into the suspension and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, excessive 5 M aqueous ethanolamine was added into this suspension until the pH reached 10, and then the pH was mediated to 6.2 by adding 2 M hydrochloric acid, to attain a yellowish-brown colloid solution. After adding CDots aqueous solution containing 20 mg of CDots and stirring 1 h, the solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 12 h. Upon cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged, washed six times with deionized water and ethanol, and collected by freeze-drying for further characterization and treatment. In contrast, a counterpart prepared under the same conditions but without the incorporation of CDots was named as rGO decorated m-BiVO4 nanosheets (BrG), other counterparts prepared under the same conditions but with the incorporation of 40 mg or 60 mg of CDots were denoted as reference samples.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum were performed on a JEOL JSM-6360LV electron microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were carried out on a JEOL JEM-ARM200F electron microscope, using the carbon-coated copper grid. The crystal structure was confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Rigaku D/max 2550VL/PC system operated at 35 kV and 200 mA with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were collected on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Mg Kα (hv = 1253.6 eV) radiation as the excitation source; the binding energies were referred to as the C 1s peak (284.8 eV) from adventitious carbon. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 spectrometer in KBr tablets. Raman spectra were measured by a LabRAM HR Laser Micro-Raman spectrometer at 514 nm. The surface hydrophilicity was examined by measuring the static contact angles of a water droplet (ca. 2 μL) on the corresponding samples with a Powereach JC2000D2 instrument. Room-temperature UV–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were undertaken with a Perkin–Elmer Lambda 750S UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer equipped with a 60 mm Hitachi integrating sphere accessory, using BaSO4 as reference. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was carried out using nitrogen adsorption apparatus. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired at room temperature on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The fluorescence lifetime was determined by recording the time-resolved PL (TRPL) decay spectra at room temperature on an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker Elexsys E580 electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer at room temperature by utilizing 5,5-dimethyl-l-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin-trapping reagent.

The transient photocurrent responses and electrochemistry impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed in a standard three-electrode cell system on a CHI 600E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, China) with a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution (pH = 7.0) as the electrolyte. In this system, indium tin oxide (ITO) glass substrate was treated by 1 M NaOH solution and cleaned with water and ethanol, respectively. Subsequently, the cleaned ITO glass substrate deposited with the above samples, platinum wires, and Ag/AgCl electrodes in saturated KCl solution were used as working electrodes, counter electrodes, and reference electrodes, respectively. For preparing a working electrode, 10 mg of sample and 500 μl of 5 wt% Nafion solution were dispersed in 5 mL of water-ethanol solution (Vwater/Vethanol = 1/4) to form a homogeneous slurry, which was then dropped and spin-coated on a 1.0 cm × 2.5 cm ITO glass substrate and exposed to air for 6 h to dry. Total working electrodes were prepared using the above process with the same concentration, coating time, and coating area, to attain the same loading for all samples. The transient photocurrent responses of samples as visible or near-infrared light switched on and off were measured at a constant potential of 0.6 V, obtained from a 300 W xenon lamp with a 420 nm or 800 nm cut-filter. The EIS measurements were carried out under a constant potential of 0.6 V from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz without light illumination, and the amplitude of the applied sine wave potential in each case was 5 mV. These measurements were carried out at room temperature.

The photocatalytic water oxidation activity of as-obtained products was tested with a PLS-SXE300 Labsolar-6A closed gas-circulation system (Perfectlight, China) in sacrificial reagents under visible or near-infrared light irradiation from a 300 W xenon lamp (Perfectlight, China) coupled with a 420 nm or 800 nm cut-filter. In a typical process, 30 mg of photocatalysts was poured into 50 mL of 0.15 M Fe(NO3)3 aqueous solution in a Pyrex top-irradiation reaction vessel equipped with a quartz lid (Perfectlight, China) at ca. 5 °C controlled by recirculating cooling water. Subsequently, the entire reaction setup was thoroughly degassed to remove air, and then the reaction system was magnetically stirred and irradiated under visible or near-infrared light. Ultimately the amount of O2 evolved was qualitatively analyzed by an online GC7890II gas chromatograph (Techcomp, China) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (5 Å molecular sieve column, He carrier gas, column temperature at 45 °C, inlet temperature at 110 °C and test temperature at 120 °C). All peaks were examined against corresponding standards, and each experiment was conducted three times in parallel to obtain an average value.

The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was measured under the same photocatalytic reaction condition, except for inserting an appropriate band-pass filter (420, 450, 475, 500, 520 nm, λ±10 nm at 10% of peak height) in front of a 300 W xenon lamp. For these tests, the light intensity was measured at eight different points to obtain an average intensity using a PL-MW2000 optical power meter (Perfectlight, China). The AQE was calculated using the following formula [27]:
A Q E =
N u m b e r
o f
r e a c t e d
h o l e s
N u m b e r
o f
i n c i d e n t
p h o t o n s
=
α × M ×
N A
× h × c
S × P × t × λ
× 100 %
Where α = 4 for the O2 evolution reaction, M is the amount of O2 molecule, N
A is the Avogadro constant, h is the Planck constant, c is the speed of light, S is the irradiation area, P is the intensity of the irradiation, t is the photoreaction time and λ is the wavelength of the monochromatic light.

As hypothesized, carbon dots (CDots) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) co-decorated monoclinic-BiVO4 nanosheets (CBrG) was constructed through a simple self-assembly process, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . In our original method, CDots were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal pathway using ethylene glycol and citric acid as the precursors, during which the internal graphitized core connected with external oxygen-containing functional groups (OFGs) formed by carbonization. The CBrG composites were then prepared by well mixing graphene oxide (GO), precursors of BiVO4, and CDots in deionized water followed by hydrothermal treatment. Firstly, the physical structure of the final products is totally depicted by SEM (Fig. 2 a and b) and TEM (Fig. 2c and d) images. Both SEM and TEM images unveil the BiVO4 owning sheet-shaped morphology is well dispersed on wrinkled rGO, the corresponding EDS spectrum demonstrates the formation of pure BiVO4 in CBrG (Fig. S1). Meanwhile, the characterization of the final products by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) shows an interplanar spacing of 2.55 Å for the (002) spacing of monoclinic-BiVO4 (insets of Fig. 2c and d). Thus, the incorporation of CDots makes no change to the main morphology and crystal structure of BiVO4 nanosheets. Although the CDots are difficult to be resolved through SEM images due to their quantum size, TEM images show the CDots highlighted by yellow circles were nonuniformly distributed on rGO (Fig. 2e and f). Even if apparent regions of dots denser by one order of magnitude than the average concentration in Fig. 2e. These images indicate that the formation of a 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid architecture between CDots and rGO. Furthermore, an HRTEM image of a CDot crystallite (inset of Fig. 2f) shows an interplanar spacing of 2.02 Å, following the (101) spacing of graphitic carbon. From the statistical analysis of the TEM image (Fig. 2e), all the visual CDots centered at ca. 2.58 nm in diameter are the typical quantum size as shown in Fig. 2g. To further reveal the crystal structure information, XRD patterns in Fig. 2h are both indexed to the monoclinic-BiVO4 (JCPDS No. 14−0688), validating that the BiVO4 is highly crystalline and phase pure. This result is consistent with the HRTEM images of BiVO4 above, excludes the impact of the incorporation of CDots on the crystal structure of BiVO4. In a previous study, the CDots show a typical peak at around 20° in XRD patterns [28]. Whereas no characteristic peaks of CDots were detected in our case, which is ascribed to the low loading amount and particularly high dispersion of CDots on rGO in CBrG.

The chemical status of both BrG and CBrG was investigated by XPS spectra. Fig. 3 a shows the XPS survey spectra, which indicates they are primarily composed of Bi, O, V, and C elements, as well as the result of the EDS spectrum (Fig. S1) above. Subsequently, the evidence for the existence of CDots in CBrG comes from C 1s XPS spectra in Fig. 3b, which is devolved into three peaks at 287.4 eV (C=O), 285.9 eV (C-O), and 284.8 eV (C=C), respectively, as a previous study [13]. The slight increase of C=O and C-O peaks as well as the atomic O/C ratio (from 0.25 to 0.34) of CBrG compared to those of BrG (Table S1) verifies the deposition of CDots, which ascertains the abundant OFGs of CDots in CBrG. As previously reported, the Bi 4f peak would display a slight shift about 0.2 eV toward higher binding energies, induced by the interaction between CDots and bismuth-based semiconductors [24]. Nevertheless, the Bi 4f spectra of both BrG and CBrG are identical (Fig. S2a). It means that CDots have been immobilized on rGO rather than BiVO4 nanosheets, which is in accord with the TEM image (Fig. S2b). The FT-IR spectra of the final products were shown in Fig. 3d. Compared with BrG, CBrG exhibits new peaks at 1728 cm−1, 1211 cm−1, and 982 cm−1, which are assigned to the stretching vibration bands of C=O, C-O-C, and C=C, respectively. This result also indicates abundant OFGs on the surface of CDots, which facilitates the dispersion and the uniform bonding of CDots on rGO. To further prove the existence of CDots, the Raman spectra of the final products have been recorded in Fig. 3e. Two characteristic peaks D and G bands are related to the sp3 hybrid carbon defects and the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms, respectively. And the intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) is used to evaluate the crystallization degree of carbon materials [29]. Obviously, the ID/IG value of CBrG increased comparing with BrG (1.08 vs. 0.91). It is acknowledged that the abundant OFGs of CDots may cause rich sp3 hybrid carbon defects (1.19 for CDots in Fig. S3). Therefore, the ID/IG value increased when it was immobilized on rGO in CBrG [30]. Given the abundant OFGs of CDots in CBrG, these hydrophilic OFGs of CDots endow the CBrG with a smaller water contact angle than the BrG (67.27° vs. 77.41°) as shown in Fig. S4. Thus, the increased hydrophily makes the CBrG well dispersed in aqueous solution.

Meanwhile, GO is converted into rGO in both BrG and CBrG as revealed by C 1s XPS spectra in Fig. S5. Compared with the GO, both BrG and CBrG present the peaks representing C=O and C-O decrease, suggesting the removal of partial OFGs of GO. To further reveal the reduction degree of rGO, the lower ID/IG values in final products (0.91 for BrG, 1.08 for CBrG) than that in pure GO (1.10 shown in Fig. S6) are observed in the Raman spectra. The result affirms that the GO in both BrG and CBrG was partially reduced. Hence, the C 1s XPS spectra along with the Raman spectra indicate that the rGO has a good reduction degree, which allows the rGO to serve as the conductive channels to facilitate the electron transfer [31]. Besides, the bands at 123, 208, 329, 366, 822 cm−1 in Fig. 3e are ascribed to the pure monoclinic phase of BiVO4 [32,33]. Notably, the C 1s XPS spectra of both BrG and CBrG are overall shifted to the lower binding energy when compared with that of GO (Fig. S5), which suggests the formation of chemical bonds between C element and a particular metal element after hydrothermal treatment [31]. Later the chemical bonds are further validated to be the V–C bonds, i.e., the two additional relatively weak peaks at the higher binding energies are observed in Fig. 3c, as reported previously [34,35]. The peaks representing the V 2p1/2 and V 2p3/2 refer to V–O bonds. Therefore, it is true that the chemical bonds formed between the BiVO4 surface and the rGO surface contribute to the interfacial electron transfer. Accordingly, the above analysis confirms that the CBrG was successfully constructed.

The incorporation of CDots in CBrG leads to an increase in the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption over the entire wavelength range investigated (Fig. 4 a). This is attributed to the strong visible-light-harvesting ability of CDots (Fig. S7), which basically arises from both n−π∗ charge-transfer transition between sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbon, and π−π∗ charge-transfer transition from sp2 carbon network [36]. Clearly, there is no change in their exterior color (inset digital photograph in Fig. 4a), which is in accord with their similar absorption edges (ca. 521 nm). The optical band gap of a semiconductor can be estimated from the Tauc plot (i.e., the curve of converted (αhv)
r vs. hv from the UV–Vis spectra, where α, h, and v are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively, and r = 2 for a direct band gap semiconductor BiVO4). Fig. 4b shows the optical band gap of CBrG is calculated to be 2.38 eV and almost identical to that of BrG (2.36 eV) within experimental error, hence they are both suitable for the visible-light-driven photocatalytic reaction. Aside from the optical band gap, we also performed XPS valence band spectra to measure their VB edge positions (Fig. 4c). The VB edge position of CBrG is +1.58 eV and almost identical to that of BrG (+1.56 eV) within experimental error. Therefore, both of their valence band (VB) edge positions satisfy the thermodynamic requirements for the photocatalytic water oxidation reaction. Based on the calculated optical band gap and the measured VB edge position above, the approximate energy band structures of both BrG and CBrG were drawn vs. a normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) in Fig. 4d. Apparently, the incorporation of CDots in CBrG has little impact on the original electronic structure.

In addition, it is true that the overall upshift of both the conduction band (CB) position (−0.80 eV for BrG, −0.80 eV for CBrG) and the VB position (1.56 eV for BrG, 1.58 eV for CBrG) compared with those of the freestanding monoclinic-BiVO4 as reported previously [37], whose CB and VB positions are identified as −0.04 eV and 2.34 eV, respectively. The higher Fermi level of rGO than that of BiVO4 causes the electron flow from the rGO to the BiVO4 until the Fermi level equilibrium is reached [38,39], leading to the formation of Mott–Schottky contact between the BiVO4 (n-type semiconductor) and the rGO (semimetal) in both BrG and CBrG. In this case, the BiVO4 absorbs photons to generate electron-hole pairs and the electrons are excited to the CB, leaving the holes in the VB. Then the thermodynamically feasible electron transfer from the CB of BiVO4 to the Fermi level of rGO is also driven by the internal electric field at their Mott–Schottky contact interface. However, the electron storage ability of the carbon network within rGO is insufficient [17], creating tremendous opportunities for the electron-hole recombination in BrG. In contrast, the CDots are immobilized on rGO in CBrG. The shift of the G band (1574 cm−1 for BrG to 1598 cm−1 for CBrG in Fig. 3e) suggests that the CDots are in the role of electron acceptors on rGO [40], i.e., the electron transfer occurs from the rGO to the CDots, facilitating the separation of electron-hole pairs. Hence for the CBrG, the photogenerated electrons from BiVO4 can be successfully separated, transferred and stored in the CDots via conductive rGO, while the holes, in turn, migrate onto the BiVO4 surface for the water oxidation reaction.

With respect to the up-conversion characteristics of CDots, the anti-Stokes PL peaks of 445–498 nm were observed in the yellowish CDots aqueous solution (Fig. 4e) under the long excitation wavelengths of 690–890 nm in Fig. 4f. The up-conversion characteristics arise from the intrinsic property of CDots, where the up-conversion PL should be assigned to a two-photon excitation process, as revealed by a previous study [19]. Intriguingly, even though the rightmost up-conversion PL peak is located at 498 nm in Fig. 4f, yet smaller than the absorption edge of CBrG (ca. 521 nm) above. That is, the up-conversion PL of CDots can mostly overlap with the absorption wavelength range of CBrG. Hence the CBrG can utilize more visible light converted from red visible and near-infrared light harvested by the incorporated CDots, which contributes to generating more electron-hole pairs to initiate water oxidation reaction compared with the BrG.

To intuitively corroborate the role of CDots in the enhancement of photocatalytic water oxidation activity, the O2 evolution experiments were carried out in a Fe(NO3)3 aqueous solution under visible or near-infrared light irradiation. Control experiments demonstrated that there was no detectable O2 generation in the dark or the absence of photocatalysts, suggesting that the O2 formation is derived from visible and/or near-infrared light driven by photocatalysts. Fig. 5 a reveals that the CBrG exhibits the O2 yield of 1008.6 μmol/g in the first 5 h under visible light irradiation, over 212% that of the BrG. Obviously, the enhanced photocatalytic water oxidation activity of CBrG strongly verified the crucial role of CDots. The increased visible light absorption led to generate more electron-hole pairs; additional electron acceptors CDots on rGO suppressed the electron-hole recombination compared with the BrG. Furthermore, the CBrG showed up to 133.0 μmol/g of O2 yield in the first 5 h under near-infrared light irradiation, while there was scarcely O2 yield of BrG under the same condition. This result indicates the up-conversion characteristics of CDots endow the CBrG with the ability to make good use of the near-infrared light for the water oxidation reaction. It is consistent with our theoretical prediction. Also, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of photocatalytic water oxidation reaction was measured as a function of the wavelength of monochromatic light (Fig. 5b). The AQE of both BrG and CBrG decreases with gradually increased wavelength, which implies that the reaction proceeds via light absorption by the photocatalysts. And the enhanced photocatalytic water oxidation activity of CBrG accounts for its higher AQE, reaching a maximum of 27.14% at 420 nm and 1.57-fold higher than that of BrG. A comparison of the AQE of CBrG with the reported records in the references is provided in Table S2, which proves that the CBrG possesses a superior water oxidation activity relative to those reported photocatalysts.

Of note, the used photocatalysts were collected and redispersed in a fresh Fe(NO3)3 aqueous solution for cycling tests to evaluate their durability. For both BrG and CBrG, Fig. 5c shows their stable O2 generation in each cycle. Although the loss of photocatalysts in the recovery process caused a decrease in oxygen evolution, the recycled CBrG still maintained over 238% that of the BrG after three cycles. Notably, the XRD patterns, the Raman spectra, and the TEM images in Figs. S8a–d together demonstrate the crystal phase and microstructure of CBrG maintained after the 15 h cycling tests, verifying it favorable reusability and photostability. To investigate the effect of CDots loading amount on the photocatalytic water oxidation activity, the photocatalysts with the CDots loading amount ranging from 20 to 60 mg (per 750 mg of m-BiVO4-rGO) were prepared as reference samples in Fig. 5d. Clearly, their photocatalytic water oxidation activity gradually decreased when the CDots loading amount exceeded 20 mg. There are usually two main reasons which may explain this downtrend of activity: (i) the aggregation of redundant CDots could cover the surface active sites; (ii) the excessive CDots could adversely turn into the recombination traps of excitons [41]. In our case, their similar Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area implies that the incorporation of CDots has little impact on the overall surface area of photocatalysts within experimental error (Fig. S9). Therefore, it is most likely that excessive CDots became the recombination centers.

Given the CBrG owning the superior photocatalytic water oxidation activity, we thereby conducted a string of control experiments to gain insight into the reaction mechanism. PL spectra were acquired to survey the pathways of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the BrG exhibits much higher fluorescence intensity than the CBrG in the range of 400–600 nm, even forming the Mott–Schottky contact between the BiVO4 and the rGO which is supposed to facilitate the charge carrier separation. Considering their only difference, it is logical to deduce that the CDots function as electron acceptors to create electron-rich regions on adjacent carbon atoms in rGO, hence hindering the electron-hole recombination and leading to such a PL quenching in CBrG. Also, the TRPL spectra were conducted to look into the electron transfer dynamics in Fig. 6b, where a biexponential fitting is applied to analyze the measured PL decay curves due to their complexity. Given the fitted parameters listed in Table S3, it is worth noting that the intensity-weighted average lifetime τav increases from 2.40 ns for BrG to 4.07 ns for CBrG. Although there are the electron transfer pathways from BiVO4 to rGO via the Mott–Schottky contact in both samples, the prolonged τav value for CBrG reconfirms that the CDots possess the electron-accepting ability on rGO and effectively suppress the electron-hole recombination. The above findings together elucidate the superiority of incorporated CDots toward the accelerated separation of electron-hole pairs, which favors the photocatalytic activity of CBrG.

Moreover, the reaction mechanism is further surveyed by PEC tests. The EIS is implemented for both BrG and CBrG in Fig. 6c, where the radius reveals the interfacial separation efficiency [42]. The smaller radius in the Nyquist plots is observed for CBrG, reflecting the CDots serve as trapping sites for capturing the electrons from photoexcited BiVO4 via conductive rGO, thus, increasing the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. This result aligns well with the PL spectra and the TRPL spectra above. Besides, the incorporation of CDots not only reduces the electron-hole recombination rate, but also offers additional light-harvesting in the visible and near-infrared light range. Hence the transient photocurrent responses were performed under visible or near-infrared light irradiation for both BrG and CBrG. Fig. 6d shows the contrast of photocurrent intensity between BrG and CBrG under visible light & near-infrared irradiation. Apparently, the photocurrent intensity of CBrG is 2.3 times that of BrG under visible light irradiation, manifesting that the incorporated CDots provide distinct improvements in the utilization of visible light for CBrG. Meanwhile, there are barely responses for BrG under near-infrared light irradiation, whereas the CBrG exhibits significant photocurrent responses under the same condition. It is also attributed to the fact that, as same as in the O2 evolution experiments, the CDots can convert near-infrared light into visible light, and subsequently excite the BiVO4 to generate photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Indeed, the multiple characterization results validate the successful incorporation of CDots on rGO makes CBrG become a potential visible and near-infrared light-driven photocatalyst.

To probe into the impact of CDots on the generated reactive oxygen species under visible light irradiation, the photoinduced molecular oxygen activation measurement was performed on both BrG and CBrG. DMPO assisted ESR inspects the spin-trapped paramagnetic oxygen species ·O2 − radicals, ·OH radicals, and ·OOH radicals. Both BrG and CBrG possess four characteristic peaks attributing to DMPO-·O2 − adduct in Fig. 6e. Here ·O2 − radicals originate from O2 reduction by electrons. It is evident that both BrG and CBrG have the visible-light-driven molecular oxygen activation ability, where the CBrG shows stronger signals than the BrG under visible light irradiation. Meanwhile, signals from the DMPO-·OH adduct along with DMPO-·OOH adduct are observed in Fig. 6f, which were formed simultaneously by a spin trap [43,44]. The ·OH radicals come from OH−/H2O oxidation by holes, while the ·OOH radicals derived from ·O2 − radicals offer conclusive evidence for the generation of ·O2 − radicals reduced from O2 [43,45]. Clearly, there are signals from two DMPO adducts observed for both BrG and CBrG. The more intense signals of CBrG indicate that the CDots endow the CBrG with more powerful molecular oxygen activation ability and water oxidation activity, in accord with the DMPO-·O2 − adduct result above. Since the number of free radicals is directly related to the peak intensity for similar systems [46], it is logical to infer that the peak intensity ratio of the DMPO-·OH adduct to the DMPO-·OOH adduct reflects the yield of holes relative to electrons. As shown in Fig. 6f, the peak intensity ratio rises substantially from BrG to CBrG, which indicates that a higher proportion of photogenerated holes involved in the molecular oxygen activation of CBrG. Namely, some of the photogenerated electrons were stored within the electron-accepting CDots via conductive rGO, others were applied for the formation of DMPO-·OOH adduct, i.e., a spin derivative of DMPO-·O2 − adduct. Whereas the majority of photogenerated holes migrated onto the BiVO4 surface for producing more DMPO-·OH adduct than DMPO-·OOH adduct in CBrG. Therefore, this analysis distinctly states that the CBrG possesses a more powerful visible-light-driven molecular oxygen activation ability as well as superior photocatalytic water oxidation activity.

Based on our characterization results, the feasible photocatalytic mechanism of CBrG can be schematically illustrated in Fig. 7 . Upon visible and near-infrared light excitation, the BiVO4 could generate plenty of electron-hole pairs with the help of CDots. The electrons are excited to the CB of BiVO4, leaving the holes in the VB of BiVO4, then the thermodynamically feasible electron transfer from the CB of BiVO4 to the Fermi level of rGO is also driven by the internal electric field at their Mott–Schottky contact interface. Immediately, the photogenerated electrons can be further separated, transferred and stored in the CDots (as electron acceptors) via conductive rGO (as electron transfer channels), while the photogenerated holes migrate onto the BiVO4 surface for the final water oxidation reaction. Overall, the distinct roles of CDots in CBrG during the photocatalytic water oxidation process can be summarized as follows: (i) with both n−π∗ and π−π∗ charge-transfer transition natures, the CDots favor absorbing increased visible light to help generate more electron-hole pairs; (ii) by virtue of the intrinsic up-conversion characteristics, the CDots are able to harvest near-infrared light to emit visible light, which in turn excites BiVO4 to form rich electron-hole pairs; (iii) the photogenerated electrons can be successfully transferred and trapped by CDots via conductive rGO, facilitating the separation of electron-hole pairs. Given the CBrG owning both the visible and near-infrared light utilization ability, and excellent photogenerated electron transfer properties, we attain prospective signs for the high-performance photocatalyst design targeting solar energy conversion.

Considering the m-BiVO4-rGO usually viewed as excellent WOPs still suffer from limited light-harvesting ability and insufficient charge carrier separation efficiency, we thus deemed it as a research platform, to demonstrate the improvement of 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid strategy on the photocatalytic water oxidation activity. Following this strategy, the ternary system had been successfully constructed with 0D CDots loaded on 2D rGO. Given the visible-light-harvesting ability and up-conversion characteristics of 0D CDots, more photogenerated electron-hole pairs can participate in water oxidation under visible and near-infrared light irradiation. Simultaneously, 0D CDots behave as electron acceptors on 2D rGO to suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs. This nature allows the feasible electron transfer from excited m-BiVO4 to electron acceptors 0D CDots via electron transfer channels of 2D rGO, facilitating the separated holes to migrate onto the m-BiVO4 surface for water oxidation. Profiting from the merits above, the ternary system CBrG exhibited the O2 yield up to 1008.6 μmol/g under visible light irradiation, over 212% that of binary system BrG, as well as the O2 yield of 133.0 μmol/g under near-infrared light irradiation, to scarcely O2 yield of BrG. Meanwhile, the CBrG reached a maximum AQE of 27.14% at 420 nm and ca. 1.57-fold higher than that of BrG. Also, the CBrG showed admirable reusability and photostability. This work not only constructs an impressive ternary photocatalyst system based on a 0D/2D all-carbon hybrid architecture for alleviating the drawbacks of m-BiVO4-rGO, but also validates the crucial role of CDots for optimizing photocatalytic water oxidation activity, opening a window for achieving superior solar energy conversion.

Siyuan Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xin Li: Validation. Xin Meng: Visualization. Tianxing Chen: Investigation. Weiyu Kong: Resources. Yao Li: Writing - review & editing. Yixin Zhao: Supervision. Dawei Wang: Supervision. Shenmin Zhu: Writing - review & editing. Waqas Akram Cheema: Formal analysis. Jian Pan: Writing - review & editing.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning the electronic structure of graphene by molecular charge transfer: a computational study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Manna AK, Pati SK. Tuning the electronic structure of graphene by molecular charge transfer: A computational study. Chemistry-An Asian Journal. 2009;4(6):855-860.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots anchored on octahedral CoO as a stable visible-light-responsive composite photocatalyst for overall water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shi W, Guo F, Zhu C, Wang H, Li H, Huang H, et al. Carbon dots anchored on octahedral CoO as a stable visible-light-responsive composite photocatalyst for overall water splitting. Journal of Materials Chemistry A. 2017;5(37):19800-19807.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as solid-state electron mediator for BiVO4/CDs/CdS Z-scheme photocatalyst working under visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu X, Zhao J, Wang L, Han M, Zhang M, Wang H, et al. Carbon dots as solid-state electron mediator for BiVO4/CDs/CdS Z-scheme photocatalyst working under visible light. Applied Catalysis B: Environmental. 2017;206:501-509.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Requirements for nitric oxide generation from isoniazid activation in vitro and inhibition of mycobacterial respiration in vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Timmins GS, Master S, Rusnak F, Deretic V. Requirements for nitric oxide generation from isoniazid activation in vitro and inhibition of mycobacterial respiration in vivo. Journal of Bacteriology. 2004;186(16):5427-5431.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evidence for the role of a peroxidase compound I-type intermediate in the oxidation of glutathione, NADH, ascorbate, and dichlorofluorescin by cytochrome c/H2O2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lawrence A, Jones CM, Wardman P, Burkitt MJ. Evidence for the role of a peroxidase compound I-type intermediate in the oxidation of glutathione, NADH, ascorbate, and dichlorofluorescin by cytochrome c/H2O2. Journal of Biological Chemistry. 2003;278(32):29410-29419.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxygen-vacancy-mediated exciton dissociation in BiOBr for boosting charge-carrier-involved molecular oxygen activation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang H, Yong D, Chen S, Jiang S, Zhang X, Shao W, et al. Oxygen-vacancy-mediated exciton dissociation in BiOBr for boosting charge-carrier-involved molecular oxygen activation. Journal of the American Chemical Society. 2018;140(5):1760-1766.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Constructing solid-gas-interfacial fenton reaction over alkalinized-C3N4 photocatalyst to achieve apparent quantum yield of 49% at 420 nm

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li Y, Ouyang S, Xu H, Wang X, Bi Y, Zhang Y, et al. Constructing solid-gas-interfacial fenton reaction over alkalinized-C3N4 photocatalyst to achieve apparent quantum yield of 49% at 420 nm. Journal of the American Chemical Society. 2016;138(40):13289-13297.
